data.title: Grupo Carso. de (BMV:GCARSOA1) Has A Somewhat Strained Balance Sheet
data.description:
David Iben put it well when he said, 'Volatility is not a risk we care about. What we care about is avoiding the permanent loss of capital.' So it might be obvious that you need to consider debt, when you think about how risky any given stock is, because too much debt can sink a company. We can see that Grupo Carso, S.A.B. de C.V. (BMV:GCARSOA1) does use debt in its business. But is this debt a concern to shareholders?

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

Why Does Debt Bring Risk?

Debt is a tool to help businesses grow, but if a business is incapable of paying off its lenders, then it exists at their mercy. Ultimately, if the company can't fulfill its legal obligations to repay debt, shareholders could walk away with nothing. While that is not too common, we often do see indebted companies permanently diluting shareholders because lenders force them to raise capital at a distressed price. Having said that, the most common situation is where a company manages its debt reasonably well - and to its own advantage. The first step when considering a company's debt levels is to consider its cash and debt together.

What Is Grupo Carso. de's Debt?

The image below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that Grupo Carso. de had debt of Mex$45.6b at the end of September 2025, a reduction from Mex$50.4b over a year. However, it also had Mex$17.3b in cash, and so its net debt is Mex$28.4b.

How Healthy Is Grupo Carso. de's Balance Sheet?

The latest balance sheet data shows that Grupo Carso. de had liabilities of Mex$64.7b due within a year, and liabilities of Mex$43.1b falling due after that. On the other hand, it had cash of Mex$17.3b and Mex$66.1b worth of receivables due within a year. So it has liabilities totalling Mex$24.4b more than its cash and near-term receivables, combined.

Of course, Grupo Carso. de has a titanic market capitalization of Mex$277.7b, so these liabilities are probably manageable. However, we do think it is worth keeping an eye on its balance sheet strength, as it may change over time.

See our latest analysis for Grupo Carso. de

We measure a company's debt load relative to its earnings power by looking at its net debt divided by its earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) and by calculating how easily its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) cover its interest expense (interest cover). Thus we consider debt relative to earnings both with and without depreciation and amortization expenses.

Looking at its net debt to EBITDA of 1.2 and interest cover of 4.8 times, it seems to us that Grupo Carso. de is probably using debt in a pretty reasonable way. So we'd recommend keeping a close eye on the impact financing costs are having on the business. Shareholders should be aware that Grupo Carso. de's EBIT was down 22% last year. If that earnings trend continues then paying off its debt will be about as easy as herding cats on to a roller coaster. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. But ultimately the future profitability of the business will decide if Grupo Carso. de can strengthen its balance sheet over time. So if you're focused on the future you can check out this free report showing analyst profit forecasts.

But our final consideration is also important, because a company cannot pay debt with paper profits; it needs cold hard cash. So the logical step is to look at the proportion of that EBIT that is matched by actual free cash flow. During the last three years, Grupo Carso. de produced sturdy free cash flow equating to 57% of its EBIT, about what we'd expect. This cold hard cash means it can reduce its debt when it wants to.

Our View

Grupo Carso. de's EBIT growth rate was a real negative on this analysis, although the other factors we considered cast it in a significantly better light. But on the bright side, its ability to handle its debt, based on its EBITDA, isn't too shabby at all. Looking at all the angles mentioned above, it does seem to us that Grupo Carso. de is a somewhat risky investment as a result of its debt. That's not necessarily a bad thing, since leverage can boost returns on equity, but it is something to be aware of. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. But ultimately, every company can contain risks that exist outside of the balance sheet. For instance, we've identified 1 warning sign for Grupo Carso. de that you should be aware of.

data.title: Biogen’s High Dose Spinraza Win Extends SMA Treatment Options In EU
data.description:
• European Commission grants marketing authorization for a high dose regimen of SPINRAZA for 5q spinal muscular atrophy.
  • The decision expands available treatment regimens for the most common form of SMA in the European Union.
  • The update adds to Biogen’s neurology-focused portfolio and regulatory progress in rare diseases.

For investors watching Biogen (NasdaqGS:BIIB), this approval aligns with the company’s core neurology focus, where rare disease therapies have been an important pillar. SPINRAZA is already an established treatment for spinal muscular atrophy, and a high dose regimen introduces another option for doctors and patients within that same indication.

This type of regulatory decision can influence how Biogen positions its SMA franchise in Europe and how treatment protocols may evolve over time. In the context of Biogen’s broader story, it serves as an additional data point on the company’s efforts to maintain relevance in neurology and rare disorders across major global markets.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Biogen by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Biogen.

How Biogen stacks up against its biggest competitors

The EC approval of a high dose SPINRAZA regimen broadens Biogen’s treatment options in 5q SMA, which represents about 95% of SMA cases, and gives the company a way to serve both newly diagnosed infants and existing patients on the lower dose. The regimen’s more rapid loading phase and less frequent maintenance dosing every four months could be relevant for long term treatment patterns, especially if physicians see the Phase 2/3 DEVOTE data as clinically meaningful.

Advertisement

Biogen narrative, rare disease focus and product depth

Even without formal narrative data, this decision sits squarely in Biogen’s long running story as a neurology and rare disease specialist, where adding dosing flexibility in an established franchise can matter for how durable that franchise looks compared with competing SMA therapies.

Risks and rewards to keep in mind

  • High dose SPINRAZA targets the most common SMA segment, which may support the relevance of Biogen’s SMA portfolio in a large share of the disease population.
  • Statistically significant motor function gains in treatment naïve infants and observed improvements in patients who switched from the low dose provide clinical support for the new regimen.
  • The label still carries procedure related and treatment related warnings, including lumbar puncture reactions, low platelet counts, clotting issues, renal toxicity and hydrocephalus, which investors should factor into risk assessments.
  • The high dose is under review by the U.S. FDA and other regulators, so future decisions outside Europe remain a source of regulatory uncertainty.

What to watch next

From here, the key things to watch are how quickly European treatment centers adopt the high dose regimen, how regulators in the U.S. and other regions rule on similar filings, and whether real world data continues to align with the DEVOTE study results. Approach 1: Ryman Hospitality Properties Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future adjusted funds from operations, then discounting those cash flows back to today in $ terms.

For Ryman Hospitality Properties, the model uses a 2 stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on adjusted funds from operations. The latest twelve month free cash flow is $527.8 million. Analysts supply estimates out to 2027, with Simply Wall St extending the projections further. By 2035, the model is using an extrapolated free cash flow figure of $901.0 million, all discounted back to present value using the model's required return assumptions.

Pulling all of those discounted cash flows together produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $206.75 per share, compared with a current share price of around $93.48. That gap implies the stock screens as roughly 54.8% undervalued based on this DCF framework.
